title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several infrastructure items including a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract with King Gravel, a resolution supporting state transportation funding, and construction service agreements for upcoming road projects. The committee also discussed sanitary sewer discharge violations and directed staff to create enforcement language with fines starting at $100 per day.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a local road improvement grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, originally a 2029 project that will likely move to 2028. The grant covers over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating, Schofield Avenue repairs by HOS Construction, and Fuller Street work by Melvin. A special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue was scheduled for Thursday, May 28th at 6:00 PM.

Staff reported road limits were removed on April 29th along with Marathon County. Activities included mailbox and sign repairs, dewatering operations near Weston Avenue tributary for approximately one week, curb line sweeping, hot mix patching, storm damage cleanup from East Nick area, inlet repairs before Birch Street project, and installation of overhead signs on Schofield Avenue. Culvert failures on Jones Street required additional 18-inch culvert installation. Equipment maintenance included removing plow equipment, servicing mowers and tractors, and preparing units for summer use.

Josh reported on water service repairs including one at Caribou Coffee, well maintenance items, and meter card maintenance. Sanitary operations saw significant increased flows due to clear water infiltration - April showed 37 million gallons pumped into the system but 53-54 million gallons treated, indicating approximately 15 million gallons of infiltration/inflow. Staff identified sump pump discharges into sanitary lines as a contributor. The INI study with in-pipe technologies will help identify problem areas. Well 2 rehab with Carrie Well started today.

Michael presented the annual report showing operating revenues of approximately $3 million, operating expenses resulting in net income of $482,911 (compared to $490,000 in 2024). Water loss was 6%. The utility value at year-end 2025 was nearly $34 million, an increase of 6.7% from infrastructure placed in service. The utility serves about 6,100 customers total - 5,250 in Weston, 830 in Rothschild, and 5 in Schofield/Rib Mountain Metro. PAS settlement funds of $1.44 million were received. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities sample resolution supports long-term funding solutions for the state transportation fund, noting that while grant funding helps one-time costs, recurring annual payments for street maintenance and snow plowing remain underfunded and require general tax dollar subsidies. Motion to recommend village board adopt the resolution passed unanimously.

Michael presented the 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract, with King Gravel as low bidder at $4.23/ton (versus $4.25 from competitor). Estimated tonnage is 30,000 tons for a total cost of $126,900. Analysis showed the village saves approximately $11/cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs ($17.11 vs $28/cubic yard), saving about $130,000 across three 2026 projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Staff sought committee guidance on enforcement of ordinance violations for residents discharging sump pumps into sanitary sewers. The code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day per violation. Committee discussed distinguishing between unknowing and willful violations. After discussion about the approximately $30,000-$42,000 in additional treatment costs from infiltration, committee directed staff to create enforcement language with fines starting at $100 per day for first offenses. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael presented an agreement for a new duplex at 2230 Radkey Avenue to temporarily connect to water and sewer mains until Radkey Avenue reconstruction. Committee added requirement that reconstruction occur no later than 2029, at which time property owner will pay assessment for main extension. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Committee approved intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for Military Road construction from Wolfman Street to Helen Street. Weston's share for utility work (sanitary sewer and water main relocation) is estimated at $94,860. Steam Construction is the contractor. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For Alderson/Jelinek intersection (roundabout), MSA was selected at $37,315 (vs Clark Dietz at $48,910). For Bladel Avenue, Clark Dietz was recommended at $73,730 (vs MSA at $70,400) due to having full-time experienced staff versus MSA's summer intern. Motion to approve both selections passed unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Recommend village board adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with $100/day fines
Schedule Bladel Avenue special assessment meeting for May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Authorize water/sewer connection agreement for 2230 Radkey Avenue with 2029 reconstruction deadline
Approve Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild ($94,860 Weston share)
Award construction services to MSA for Alderson/Jelinek project and Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water infiltration treatment costs for next meeting
Look at adding street lighting to Schofield Avenue from Birch Street to Camp Phillips Road in 2030 project
Norcon to begin concrete repairs on Schofield Avenue eastbound lanes starting Wednesday/Thursday

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its reorganization meeting, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission noted that a competing balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from April 21st meeting
New business - Grant request for Taste and Glow Balloonfest

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated and elected as Chair unanimously. Cushing was nominated and elected as Vice Chair with Jackson providing the second. Renee was nominated and elected as Secretary. All elections passed unanimously by voice vote.
Minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. Motion was seconded by Renee and passed unanimously by voice vote.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill (3308 Green Pastures Lane) presented on behalf of the event, reporting approximately 11,000-12,000 attendees last year. He announced that the competing Marshfield balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making Taste and Glow the sole hot air balloon event in the area. Commission members noted the application showed 20 fewer hotel rooms than last year while requesting the same funding. A commissioner requested better tracking of hotel room usage for future applications. The commission approved a $5,000 grant (same as last year), with Jackson seconding the motion. Passed unanimously.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described meeting the candidates, including winner Anastasia, and noted the contestants were impressed with Marathon County hospitality. This was the first time the event was hosted in Marathon County since the 1970s.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant for Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Taste and Glow organizers to provide accurate hotel room usage data in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection districts. Both items passed unanimously with the commission addressing building orientation challenges related to a gas pipeline and establishing twice-yearly inspections for salt storage facilities near municipal wells.

agenda.item: Breath and communication disclosure and recusal
Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update report
Future agenda items and staff updates

discussions.item: Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility site plan
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. The building was rotated to face east due to a gas pipeline running under the parking lot area, which reduced fill costs significantly. Chief Jeremy explained the building will accommodate 50-60 officers (current staffing is 40 officers plus 4 clerical). An alternate storage shed is proposed but may be built later depending on bid costs. Discussion included future expansion possibilities to the north and positioning the shed to avoid future relocation. Commissioner Zagami moved to recommend approval subject to staff conditions 2-5. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Staff proposed a new inspection program with two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focused on structural integrity and a winter operational inspection focused on material handling and containment. A standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards was developed. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg that predates the wellhead protection ordinance and has never gone through the conditional use process. The commission agreed staff should reach out to bring them into compliance. Commissioner Lumper praised the well-organized program. Motion to approve with reporting back to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee passed unanimously.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
discussions.body: Motion to approve passed unanimously with no discussion.
Motion to approve passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Planning and development department monthly project update
discussions.body: Staff announced the first meeting of the joint plan commission and village board steering committee for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Staff will send calendar invites. Discussion arose about residential TIF districts for workforce housing, with staff noting Ehlers has prepared a presentation on qualifications that could be shared with commissioners or incorporated into comprehensive plan discussions.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke remotely. He asked the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project, referencing previous relaxed standards for the Greenheck Turner building to avoid unnecessary taxpayer expense. He also requested the commission require annual reporting on the salt shed inspection program to ensure complaints don't get 'lost in the sauce' as with some other zoning complaints.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court at 3600 Community Center Drive approved and recommended to Board of Trustees subject to staff conditions 2-5
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted for wellhead protection district with twice-yearly inspections
Staff to report inspection findings to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee next year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility on Sternberg that predates wellhead protection ordinance
Joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send email and calendar invites for steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging station regulations added to future agenda for fall consideration
Next regular meeting scheduled for Monday, June 8th, 2026 at 6:00 p.m.

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an introductory meeting for new board members, featuring educational presentations from the Health Department, Veterans Service Office, and Aging Disability Resource Center. The committee approved April 1st meeting minutes unanimously and received overviews of department missions, budgets, and current initiatives including homelessness response efforts and veteran suicide prevention programs.

agenda.item: Call to order and remote attendance confirmation
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute - no items for this meeting
Review of standing committee ordinance section 2.04 - missions, duties, and responsibilities
Health Department presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Lance Leonard
Veterans Service Office presentation by VSO Aaron Galindo
Aging Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray

discussions.item: Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion carried unanimously with all members voting in favor.

Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee's jurisdiction over the Health Department, Social Services Department, Veterans Service Department, North Central Health Care, and the Aging Disability Resource Center. He detailed the various advisory boards that report to the committee including the Social Services Board, Veterans Service Commission, Transportation Coordinating Committee, and Board of Health. Chair Boots noted the committee will be working on a new strategic plan with completion targeted for December.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented on the department's mission of preventing disease, promoting health, and protecting from environmental hazards. The department has 37.6 FTE staff across four teams: environmental health (overseeing 1,200+ licenses), nursing/communicable disease, community health improvement, and administration. She highlighted the new homeless system response position funded by the county board, public health vending machines providing free tick kits and other supplies, and the Nurse Family Partnership program which just graduated its first participants after three years.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa who was unavailable. The department has approximately 140 FTE and a 2026 budget of over $22 million with about $7 million in tax levy. He highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program that addresses root causes of housing instability for child welfare and youth justice cases. Leonard indicated potential restructuring requests coming for staffing as some pilot programs have shown results and some units have seen increased demand.

VSO Aaron Galindo reported his office increased from 2.75 to 3.0 FTE and handled 8,149 interactions in 2025, up from 5,900 the prior year. He reported $44 million in annual VA benefits flowing to Marathon County veterans, with $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Galindo highlighted suicide prevention efforts noting Marathon County had 30 suicides in 2025 with 7 being veterans. He's exploring the Staff Sergeant Gordon Fox federal suicide prevention grant. The office is also working with Center for Veterans Issues on housing stability and has established a local SSVF grant office for unhoused veterans.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the ADRC serves four counties (Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood) with about 60 FTE staff and 385-400 volunteers. The organization receives 11% of its budget from county levy, with Marathon County contributing $413,135. Ray reported significant financial challenges as federal funding has remained flat since 2024 while nutrition program costs increased 40-45% over four years. They've had to reduce meals served, service days, and staffing. The ADRC hadn't requested a levy increase from 2011 until 2024.

actionItems: April 1st meeting minutes approved unanimously
North Central Healthcare will be scheduled for a separate full presentation before the committee
ADRC Executive Director Mike Ray to present annual report with 2025 data to full board
Next meeting corrected to Tuesday June 3rd at 3:00 PM (not May 29th as originally stated)
Committee members to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with suggested agenda items for future meetings
Chair Boots to bring strategic plan items to upcoming meetings

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees for new residential development, passed several ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and discussed ongoing challenges with the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, pledge of allegiance, roll call
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Approval of minutes from previous meetings
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Consent agenda - vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, e-bikes, and transportation funding resolution
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Public Hearing - Park and Recreation Impact Fees
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed increases to park and recreation impact fees from $300 to $761 for single family units, $250 to $563 for duplex units, and $250 to $495 for multifamily units, consistent with the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke during public comment criticizing the lack of detailed operational data justifying the fees. Trustee Motti supported the increase, noting population growth requires more park equipment and space. Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami, passed unanimously. Resolution 2026-015 was approved with motion by Hermling, second by Clark, passed unanimously.

Ordinance 26-008 creates a reciprocal fire inspection agreement with Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, Clover, Riverside, and Safer. Chief Finkey explained that food trucks inspected by any participating department receive a sticker valid across all jurisdictions, eliminating redundant inspections. Motion by Lope, second by Zagami, passed unanimously.

The board debated amendments to section 1.08 of the elected officials handbook. Discussion centered on removing specific requirements about health, safety, and welfare justifications for remote attendance, keeping the policy broad. Trustee Beck moved to amend by striking sentences requiring specific reasons for remote attendance. The amendment passed with Hermling and Motti voting no. The original motion as amended then passed with the same two dissenting votes.

President appointed Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, second by Lopes, passed unanimously. The board agreed to include future committee applicant resumes in board packets. Parks, Finance/HR, and CLIPS still have openings.

Board discussed challenges with the farmers market's relocation from the Camp Phillips/Scoffield corner to Kennedy Park after the property owner developed that site. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about cars parking on grass along Alderson. Renee explained the market grew from 25 to 53 vendor spots and vendors appreciate having vehicles with them. Discussion included potential alternative locations like Mont Miller Park, but concerns about visibility and attendance were raised. No action was taken; this was discussion only.

Public Works Director Michael Dolan explained the difficulty filling the engineering technician position, noting the specific skill set required is scarce in central Wisconsin. Even consulting firms are pulling staff from southeast Wisconsin. Two contracts for outside engineering inspection services totaling approximately $108,000 were approved for projects starting in June.

Michael Dolan reported that a speed study showed the 85th percentile speed on Weston Avenue was 45 mph. The original engineering study recommended 35 mph. A second study is needed but was delayed due to construction. The matter will return to the board, possibly at a joint meeting.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 2052 Street offered public comment criticizing the hiring of outside engineering consultants for $108,000 when an engineering technician position remains unfilled. He questioned why Lisa Beck was introduced as a Safer board member at the April Fire Department meeting before her official appointment. He also criticized the lack of a detailed Kennedy Park report in the meeting packet and called for greater administrator accountability. Pitel also commented during the park impact fee hearing, stating there is no solid operational data justifying the fee amounts.
actionItems: Park and recreation impact fees increased effective immediately per Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015
Mobile food vendor reciprocal inspection ordinance approved
E-bike and nuisance ordinances approved
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove specific justification requirements
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Secondhand dealer license for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC approved contingent on background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit
Compliance assurance plan approved
Comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1 at 6:00 PM
Staff to include committee applicant resumes in future board packets
Weston Avenue speed limit study to return to board at future meeting
Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors approved committee appointments for the new term, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the Executive Committee, and unanimously passed a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees for storm debris following the recent tornado and flooding disaster. The board suspended its rules to fast-track the tipping fee resolution in response to the April severe weather emergency.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Roll call and sign-in (technical difficulties with meeting manager)
Committee appointments presentation by Chair Gibbs
Election of at-large Executive Committee members
Consent agenda items C10 through C14A
Resolution D15 - Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution D16 - Amend social services budget
Resolution E17 - Landfill tipping fee modification for storm debris
Vote to suspend rules for tipping fee resolution

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, emphasizing goals of increasing diverse perspectives, mentoring newer supervisors (34% of board in first or second term), and honoring supervisor preferences. The appointments included both county board chair and administrator appointments. Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims. The motion carried but was not unanimous. Supervisor Enzy noted technical issues prevented her vote and clarified she was an 'aye'.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Supervisor Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg explained her nomination of Supervisor Leur, citing her advocacy for the county's youngest and oldest residents. Paper ballots were distributed and supervisors selected two names. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the Executive Committee.
Items C10 through C14A were approved on consent. Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Resolution D15 - Out of Home Placement Reserve Increase
discussions.body: Resolution 24-26 was presented from the Health and Human Services Committee and Human Resources and Finance and Property Committee. Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Resolution D16 - Social Services Budget Amendment
discussions.body: Resolution 25-26 to amend the 2026 social services budget was presented. Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Resolution E17 - Landfill Tipping Fee Modification
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented this emergency resolution following severe weather damage. The state declared emergency waives $13.10 of fees, Ringle Township is waiving their $2.33 fee, and the county proposes reducing rates to $36.94/ton (commercial rate minus state and local waivers) to cover basic costs. Public comment was allowed from Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, who requested complete fee waivers citing ongoing insurance delays and underinsured residents. Leonard noted discussions with the Community Foundation about reimbursing uninsured debris costs. The board voted unanimously to suspend rules to bring the resolution forward, then unanimously approved Resolution 26-26.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special dispensation to speak on the tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty requested Marathon County wave its fees entirely for six months, noting the state, DNR, and Ringle are all waiving their fees, and emphasized that private businesses have donated $40,000+ in cleanup efforts while Marathon County has provided limited direct financial assistance. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of approximately 44 homes facing demolition, requesting fee waivers especially for underinsured residents still waiting on insurance adjusters and structural engineers.
actionItems: Committee appointments for the term approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to Executive Committee at-large positions
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees to $36.94/ton for certified storm debris through June 17, 2025
Administrator Leonard to continue discussions with Community Foundation regarding reimbursement mechanism for uninsured debris disposal costs
Solid Waste Management Board to develop long-term policy on handling disaster-related tipping fee reductions within six months
Supervisors to be notified of future Solid Waste Management Board meetings for attendance

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution for tornado-affected properties to the county board. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Urban Residential/General Agriculture to Rural Residential, Town of Green Valley)
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture, Town of McMillan)
Consideration of fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation: Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation: Conservation Planning and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Town of Green Valley)
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pago presented the staff report for rezoning from Urban Residential and General Agriculture to Rural Residential for a proposed land division. The Town of Green Valley Town Board recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public comment was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and the town's approval, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. The motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Town of McMillan)
discussions.body: Garrett Pago presented the petition to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature of the property; Pago explained it currently has a home and horse riding stable, and the rezone would make the home a conforming use rather than non-conforming. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No natural area is being lost. Supervisor Sefelt moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. The motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic system fees for tornado-affected properties. Administrator Lance Leonhard noted this was in response to the governor's emergency declaration. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concern about fairness to those affected by non-declared disasters. Marilyn Bend asked about septic hookup fees. After discussion, Supervisor Drebeck moved to amend the resolution to only waive reconnect fees ($160) for septic systems, not other zoning or septic fees. The amendment was seconded by Supervisor Kurt and passed. The amended resolution was then approved to forward to the county board, though the vote was not unanimous.

Director David Hagen Busher provided an overview of the department's operations, including the landfill history, tipping fees, renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy, and upcoming leachate treatment requirements by 2032. He noted hauling costs for April were close to $90,000 due to significant rainfall. The department has 11 FTE staff handling approximately 2,000 tons daily.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation and Forestry Department presentation
discussions.body: County Forest Administrator Tom Lovelin presented on behalf of Director Jamie who had an emergency. He explained the department operates both county and city park systems since 1924, manages 30,700 acres of county forest, and employs 44 full-time and 120 seasonal workers. A Nine Mile County Forest study showed out-of-town visitors generate $3.25 million for the county economy. Supervisor Sefelt announced Tom Lovelin is retiring June 1st after nearly 30 years of service.

actionItems: Rezoning from Urban Residential and General Agriculture to Rural Residential approved for James Offer and David Tract property in Town of Green Valley - forwarded to county board
Rezoning from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture approved for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz property in Town of McMillan - forwarded to county board
Modified fee waiver resolution (reconnect fees only) for April 17, 2026 tornado-affected properties forwarded to county board for consideration
Staff to track costs and requests related to fee waiver program for future policy development
Joe Tucker selected as new County Forest Administrator to replace retiring Tom Lovelin

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee approved installation of new kayak launch signs along the Eau Claire River Trail and deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center until next month. Significant public comment criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time and questioned Friends of Kennedy Park financial transparency, while staff provided updates on Kennedy Park fundraising progress and trail maintenance challenges.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail discussion
Yellow Banks kayak launch signs discussion and action

discussions.body: Minutes were approved with a motion and second. No discussion occurred. Motion carried unanimously with all present voting in favor.

Sean Osterbrink presented a timeline document showing the Kennedy Park master plan process from 2023 to present, including the May 9th ribbon cutting for Ray Jack and Western Fields. Committee member Scott asked about village capital funds, and Osterbrink explained that infrastructure funds were pulled pending the master plan development. The Friends of Kennedy Park are fundraising for field lights, currently at approximately $85,000 toward a $117,000 goal, and plan a public check presentation at their June tournament. Acting chair noted Friends of Kennedy Park are volunteers deserving grace and encouraged community involvement.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff presented photos and a PDF showing locations of trees removed from the river and the installation of wood mats over gas pipelines to protect them during equipment access. Dan explained the mats were provided by TC Energy (formerly ATC/ANR pipeline) and the village retained extra mats for future trail projects. The work was completed during winter using snow bridges to access the area.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: Discussion was deferred to next month because Committee Chair Katrina was absent due to an unforeseen issue. A resident named Andy had appeared specifically to oppose the idea, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' His objection was noted for the record, and he indicated he may return next month.

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail
discussions.body: The acting chair raised concerns about graffiti and dirty conditions on memorial benches along the trail. Osterbrink explained challenges include inability to get pressure washing equipment to the remote location and limited effectiveness of graffiti removers on concrete. Committee discussed potential solutions including anti-vandalism coatings, seal coating, and volunteer work days. No formal action was taken; staff will investigate options.

Dan Kehoe from 2625 Northwestern Avenue presented version seven of proposed kayak launch signs for installation at three Weston locations (Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks). Signs include maps, QR codes for river gauge information, and historical content specific to each location. The Highway J launch will likely be closed all season due to bridge replacement. Motion to approve village installation of the signs passed unanimously.

Committee agreed to bring back dog day at aquatic center discussion. Osterbrink requested interest in adding food allergy awareness signage at playgrounds after receiving an email request; committee agreed to add this for future discussion. Staff announced the Mock Miller Park master plan process will begin with a kickoff meeting, and the final bike and pedestrian plan open house is June 11th from 5-7 p.m.

publicComment: Two speakers provided public comment. Andy (6504 [street inaudible]) spoke in person opposing dogs at the aquatic center, stating animals should not be allowed in public pools and suggesting dogs belong in rivers and lakes. Jim Pensan (5002 [street] Street, Weston), speaking online under the name 'Vern M,' criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation, questioned the Kennedy Park funding report showing exactly $8,700 in receipts and expenditures as 'too coincidental,' asked about the $10,000 grant and Glow event proceeds, and accused Friends of Kennedy Park of being 'convoluted and misleading' with finances.
actionItems: Approved installation of Eau Claire River kayak launch signs at three Village of Weston locations (Highway J, Ross Avenue, Yellow Banks) - to be forwarded to Village Board
Dog Day at aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting when Chair Katrina can participate
Staff to investigate graffiti removal and cleaning options for Eau Claire River Trail benches
Staff to research food allergy awareness playground signage for future committee discussion
Mock Miller Park master plan kickoff meeting scheduled with Rettler planning firm
Next meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged budget reports before receiving an extensive educational presentation on the Municipal Clerk's office operations, elections, licensing, and budget. Committee member Evan Arndt announced his resignation as he is moving out of the village.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
Acknowledge work product transmittals - April budget status report
TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's budget and office overview
Old business, new business, staff remarks

discussions.item: Approval of minutes from previous meeting
discussions.body: Motion to approve made by Ernling, seconded by Daniels. No discussion. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports
discussions.body: Jessica from finance reported that TIFF bond payoff is anticipated for October-November. The CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds, expected this summer. Staff confirmed the final numbers look good and the village will be able to pay off the debt. The bonds are due October 1st.
Motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIFF reports, and legal details report made by Daniels, seconded by Soaper. Motion carried unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented an extensive overview of clerk's office operations. The office has reduced from four full-time staff to two full-time and one part-time employee. Key topics included: elections budget (higher in even years due to presidential/gubernatorial elections), Badger Book electronic poll book replacements at $11,000 for four units, 84% of tax collection ($22 million) handled at the clerk's counter, pet licensing brought back in-house saving $5,495 annually, and the quarterly newsletter 'The Wire' costing approximately $30,000 annually for 5,089 postal patrons. Discussion occurred about poll worker wages, with chief inspectors paid below average ($13/hour vs. $15 average). The pet licensing fee increase from $10 to $20 was explained as driven by Marathon County's request to cover Humane Society costs.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market at Kennedy Park has been going well. She has been there the last three Saturdays helping vendors get situated. The market is full with food trucks, coffee vendors, craft people, and greenhouse vendors. Produce vendors expected mid to late June. Concerns were raised about parking on Alderson and Newport streets. Mountain Bay Metro has been monitoring the situation.

Committee member Evan Arndt announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though staying in the metro area. He thanked the committee for his time serving.

actionItems: CDA resolution needed this summer to call TIFF bonds due October 1st
Clerk planning to propose chief inspector wage increase to $15/hour for next election term
Recruit new committee members to fill open spots on Finance and HR Committee and other committees
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee addressed several ordinance changes and code enforcement matters. Key actions included recommending approval of nuisance code amendments, an e-bike ordinance with restrictions on emotos, mobile food vendor fire inspections, and a raze order for a deteriorated property on Shore Avenue. The committee also discussed but deferred action on overnight parking regulations.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
E-bicycle ordinance in Chapter 82 Traffic and Vehicles
Mobile food vendors fire inspection amendment

discussions.body: Jim Senalt addressed the committee with concerns about the new farmers market location at Kennedy Park, stating 'my personal opinion is it's a terrible execution' and predicting the grassy areas will be destroyed. He raised concerns about vehicles parking on grass potentially violating village code, ADA-compliant handicap parking being placed on grass instead of asphalt, the new fire inspection requirement for mobile food vendors, and questioned accountability on long-standing code violations.

Audrey reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement roll after the property sale was completed and the new owner submitted repair permits. Chief Hunt reported 2,394 police calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations issued. The department has hired its 40th officer, with the 38th starting recently, 39th starting in May, and 40th around January. Two SROs work on zoning violations during summer for Weston, Rothschild, and Scofield. Reports were acknowledged unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments including tree limb height requirements (14 and 10 feet over streets and walkways) that were adjusted at the previous meeting. Motion to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees was made by Olsen, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

Extensive discussion occurred regarding emotos (electric mini motorcycles). Chief Hunt strongly advocated prohibiting emotos on roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes and citing safety concerns. The committee debated electric scooters, with concerns raised about restricting children's transportation. Motion by Olsen to recommend approval with the addition of prohibiting emotos on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property was seconded by Sherwanka. Motion passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour parking restriction (4am-4pm) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities which typically have 4-5 hour windows. Chief Hunt preferred keeping overnight restrictions to allow officers time to clear vehicles before morning plowing. Michael Wodalski indicated he could identify 6-8 specific problem areas for targeted signage. The committee deferred action, directing Michael to prepare logistics on specific exception areas for the July meeting.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between the church and DC Everest High School parking lot where parishioners park on Sundays. Staff estimated $2,000-3,000 for a basic crosswalk or $25,000-30,000 for a lighted system. The committee was reluctant to set precedent for paying for private requests. Motion by Olsen to recommend board approval if Mount Olive covers installation costs was seconded by Sherwanka and passed unanimously.

Chief Hunt explained a new regional fire inspection program with Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and SAFER. One inspection with a sticker is valid across all participating municipalities. Wausau opted out to maintain their own program. Fire Marshal Marty already conducted one inspection that failed due to improper LP piping and unsecured propane containers. Motion by Sherwanka to approve Ordinance 26-8, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

Staff presented three address corrections: 5915 Delicoski Street needs renumbering as a duplex (5915 and 5917), Ross Avenue addresses need resequencing due to an existing apartment building addressed as 3909, and 2211 Radkey Avenue needs correction for a new duplex on a private drive. Chief Hunt added that Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski has eight separate addresses for one structure. Motion passed unanimously to recommend approval to the Board.

Staff requested authorization to proceed with a raze order for a structure abandoned since 2010-2011 that was previously deemed too deteriorated for a fire training burn. The property had housed chickens inside the structure. Current owners are up to date on taxes and preferred this approach. Demolition costs will be placed on the tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding concerns about the new farmers market location at Kennedy Park, criticizing vehicle parking on grass, ADA parking placement on grass instead of asphalt, the new fire inspection requirement for food trucks, and lack of accountability on long-standing code violations.
actionItems: Recommend approval of Chapter 50 Nuisances amendments to Board of Trustees
Recommend approval of e-bicycle ordinance with emotos prohibited on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property
Michael Wodalski to prepare specific street locations for targeted parking restrictions for July meeting
Michael Wodalski to contact Mount Olive Church regarding paying for crosswalk installation
Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections approved for recommendation to Board
Address corrections for three properties recommended for Board approval
Staff authorized to begin raze order process for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property at Sternberg and South Timber with furniture and dumpster outside

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan

Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff

title: Water Works Commission Meeting

The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers

The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
